Chase Bank Analytics

Khushi Panchal · Associate Banker

SQL PROJECT 2024
Project
12 Queries
Final
Project Overview

Chase Bank Transaction Analytics

As an Associate Banker at Chase, I see hundreds of customer transactions every day. I wanted to understand the data behind those transactions — so I built this complete SQL analytics system to analyze transaction patterns, peak hours, branch performance and customer behavior. This project is directly inspired by what I observe at work every day.

4
Tables Built
10K
Transactions
12
SQL Queries
2
Months of Data
Main Business Questions Answered
Q01

Transactions Per Minute

Which minute of the day has the most activity? How many unique customers transact every minute?

→
Q02

Busiest Hours

Which hour processes the most transactions? How much money moves each hour?

→
Q03

Channel Breakdown

ATM vs Online vs Mobile vs In-Branch — which channel do customers prefer?

→
Q04

Top Customers (JOIN)

Who are Chase's most active customers and how much did they transact?

→
Q05

Failed Transaction Rate

Which branches have the highest failure rates? Where is the most risk?

→
Q06

WHERE Filter Drill Down

Show all transactions at a specific branch, on a specific date and time window.

→
Q07

NYC Payments at 11PM

Who made payments at New York branches on March 1st at 11PM?

→
Q08

Customer History (2 JOINs)

Complete spending history for any customer — where, when, how much.

→
Why I Built This
Real inspiration from real work
Every day as an Associate Banker at Chase I observe transaction patterns, customer behavior and branch operations. This project translates those real-world observations into data — using SQL to answer the questions I've always wondered about. The data is synthetic to protect customer privacy, but the business questions are 100% real.
Database Design

4 Tables That Work Together

A relational database where every table connects using Primary Keys (PK) and Foreign Keys (FK). The transactions table is the heart of the project — it links to customers, branches and accounts through foreign key relationships.

👤customersWHO is transacting? — 50 rows
customer_id PKnameaccount_typecityjoin_date
🏦branchesWHERE did it happen? — 10 rows
branch_id PKbranch_namecitystate
💸transactions ⭐ CORE TABLEWHAT happened? — 10,000 rows
txn_id PKcustomer_id FKbranch_id FKtxn_typechannelamounttxn_timestampstatus
💰accountsWhat is the balance? — 100 rows
account_id PKcustomer_id FKaccount_typeaccount_numberbalancecreated_date
How The Tables Connect
Foreign Keys Create Relationships
transactions.customer_id links to customers.customer_id — so I can show customer names instead of just numbers. transactions.branch_id links to branches.branch_id — so I can show branch names and cities. This is what makes JOIN queries possible.

Query 01 of 08

Which minute of the day has the most transactions?

Groups all 10,000 transactions by minute, counts unique customers and total transactions per minute, then ranks from busiest to quietest using ORDER BY DESC.

SQL Concepts Used
SELECTDATE_FORMATCOUNT DISTINCTCOUNTGROUP BYORDER BY DESCAS (alias)
Key Finding
07:10 AM — Busiest Minute of the Day
17 unique customers completed 19 transactions in a single 60-second window. Evening hours 8–10PM also showed consistent peak activity.
Analysis & Insights

"My first query answers the core question of the project — how many customers are transacting every single minute at Chase. I used DATE_FORMAT to extract just the hour and minute from each timestamp, COUNT DISTINCT to count unique customers without double counting anyone, and GROUP BY to create one row per minute. ORDER BY DESC puts the busiest minutes at the top. I found that 07:10 AM was the single busiest minute with 17 customers and 19 transactions — this tells Chase exactly when transaction traffic peaks so they can plan real-time monitoring and capacity."

Result Screenshot
SQL Code
USE chase_bank; SELECT DATE_FORMAT(txn_timestamp, '%h:%i %p') AS txn_minute, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS unique_customers, COUNT(txn_id) AS total_transactions FROM transactions GROUP BY txn_minute ORDER BY total_transactions DESC;
Result

1 row per minute

Covers all 1,440 minutes of the day ranked busiest first

Business Value

Real-time monitoring

Helps Chase identify peak transaction minutes for staffing and capacity planning

Why COUNT DISTINCT?

No double counting

If one customer does 3 transactions in 1 minute, they still count as 1 unique customer

Query 02 of 08

Which hour of the day is the busiest at Chase?

Groups transactions by hour, counts unique customers and total transactions, and sums up total money processed per hour. Shows which hours Chase is busiest and how much money moves.

SQL Concepts Used
SELECTDATE_FORMATCOUNT DISTINCTSUMROUNDGROUP BYORDER BY DESC
Key Finding
3 AM — Busiest Hour Processing ,216,112
462 transactions worth over .2M processed in one hour. This is peak digital banking time — customers at home using online and mobile banking after hours.
Analysis & Insights

"This query groups all transactions by hour of the day to find when Chase is busiest. I added SUM of amounts so I can see not just how many transactions happened each hour, but how much total money was processed. Surprisingly 3AM was the busiest hour — this makes complete sense because that's peak online and mobile banking time. Physical branches close at 5PM, but digital banking never stops. This kind of insight helps Chase plan server capacity and digital infrastructure around peak hours."

Result Screenshot
SQL Code
USE chase_bank; SELECT DATE_FORMAT(txn_timestamp, '%h %p') AS hour_of_day, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS unique_customers, COUNT(txn_id) AS total_transactions, ROUND(SUM(amount), 2) AS total_amount FROM transactions GROUP BY hour_of_day ORDER BY total_transactions DESC;
Result

24 rows

One row per hour of the day, busiest hour at top

Business Value

Infrastructure planning

Chase can plan server capacity, maintenance windows and staffing around peak hours

Real World Insight

Digital banking peaks at night

3AM busy = online/mobile banking, not branch visits. Shows digital shift.

Query 03 of 08

ATM vs Online vs Mobile vs In-Branch — who wins?

Breaks down all 10,000 transactions by channel type, calculating total transactions, unique customers, total money and average transaction amount per channel.

SQL Concepts Used
SELECTCOUNT *COUNT DISTINCTSUMAVGROUNDGROUP BYORDER BY DESC
Key Finding
Online Banking Leads — 2,582 Transactions, .67M
Online → 2,582 | In-Branch → 2,567 | Mobile → 2,433 | ATM → 2,418. All channels are close, but digital leads — showing Chase's digital investment is paying off. Average transaction around ,560 across all channels.
Analysis & Insights

"Query 3 breaks down all transactions by channel — ATM, Online, Mobile and In-Branch. I used COUNT star to count all transactions, COUNT DISTINCT for unique customers, SUM for total money, and AVG for the average transaction size. Online banking came out on top with 2,582 transactions and .67M processed. All 4 channels are very close though, which tells Chase that customers are actively using every channel. As someone who works at the branch, I actually see this — customers use the app AND come in person. This data proves it."

Result Screenshot
SQL Code
USE chase_bank; SELECT txn_type AS transaction_channel, COUNT(*) AS total_transactions, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS unique_customers, ROUND(SUM(amount), 2) AS total_amount, ROUND(AVG(amount), 2) AS avg_transaction_amount FROM transactions GROUP BY txn_type ORDER BY total_transactions DESC;
Result

4 clean rows

One row per channel — easy to compare at a glance

Business Value

Channel investment strategy

Shows Chase where to invest — digital vs physical, app vs website

Mobile vs Online

Not the same!

Mobile = Chase app on your phone. Online = chase.com on your laptop. Chase tracks both separately.

Query 04 of 08

Which customers are the most active?

Uses JOIN to connect the transactions table with the customers table — showing real customer names, account types and cities instead of just ID numbers. Ranked by most transactions.

SQL Concepts Used
SELECTJOINONCOUNTSUMAVGROUNDGROUP BYORDER BY DESCtable aliases (t, c)
Key Finding
Zoey Mitchell — Most Active Customer — 232 Transactions, 00,398
Checking account customer from Atlanta. Jack Walker (LA, 227 txns) and Sophia White (New York, 223 txns) closely behind. This query uses JOIN — without it, we'd only see customer_id numbers, not names.
Analysis & Insights

"This is where JOIN comes in. The transactions table only stores customer_id — a number. To see actual customer names I had to JOIN the customers table ON the matching customer_id column. Now instead of seeing 'customer 38 did 232 transactions', I see 'Zoey Mitchell did 232 transactions totaling 00,398'. I used table aliases — t for transactions and c for customers — to keep the code clean. This query directly answers who Chase's VIP customers are, which is valuable for loyalty programs and premium service targeting."

Result Screenshot
SQL Code
USE chase_bank; SELECT c.name AS customer_name, c.account_type, c.city, COUNT(t.txn_id) AS total_transactions, ROUND(SUM(t.amount), 2) AS total_amount, ROUND(AVG(t.amount), 2) AS avg_amount FROM transactions t JOIN customers c ON t.customer_id = c.customer_id GROUP BY c.name, c.account_type, c.city ORDER BY total_transactions DESC;
Result

50 rows

One row per customer with full name, city, transaction count and total amount

Business Value

VIP customer identification

Chase can use this to identify premium customers for loyalty rewards, personal banker assignment and targeted offers

What is JOIN?

Connecting two tables

JOIN links transactions to customers wherever customer_id matches — like a VLOOKUP in Excel but much more powerful

Query 05 of 08

Which branch has the highest failed transaction rate?

Connects transactions to branches via JOIN, uses CASE WHEN to count only failed transactions, then calculates the failure percentage for each branch. Risk monitoring query.

SQL Concepts Used
JOINCOUNTCASE WHEN THEN ENDROUNDGROUP BYORDER BY DESCpercentage calculation
Risk Alert — Key Finding
Chase Midtown New York — 20.71% Failure Rate
209 failed out of 1,009 total transactions. 1 in 5 transactions failed — a serious red flag. Chase South Beach Miami was the safest at 18.29% failure rate.
Analysis & Insights

"Query 5 is my risk monitoring query — and it's the one I'm most proud of. I used JOIN to connect transactions to branches so I can see branch names. Then I used CASE WHEN — which is like an IF statement in SQL — to count only the failed transactions out of the total. Dividing failed by total and multiplying by 100 gives me the failure percentage. Chase Midtown New York had the highest failure rate at 20.71% — that's 1 in 5 transactions failing. In a real bank, this would trigger an immediate investigation into the branch's technical systems. This is exactly the kind of analysis a Business Tools Analyst does every day."

Result Screenshot
SQL Code
USE chase_bank; SELECT b.branch_name, b.city, b.state, COUNT(t.txn_id) AS total_transactions, COUNT(CASE WHEN t.status = 'failed' THEN 1 END) AS failed_transactions, COUNT(CASE WHEN t.status = 'success' THEN 1 END) AS successful_transactions, ROUND(COUNT(CASE WHEN t.status = 'failed' THEN 1 END) * 100.0 / COUNT(t.txn_id), 2) AS failure_rate_pct FROM transactions t JOIN branches b ON t.branch_id = b.branch_id GROUP BY b.branch_name, b.city, b.state ORDER BY failure_rate_pct DESC;
Result

10 rows

One row per branch ranked by failure rate — highest risk at top

Business Value

Risk monitoring

Identifies branches with technical problems needing immediate investigation — protects customers and revenue

What is CASE WHEN?

SQL's IF statement

CASE WHEN status = 'failed' THEN 1 END — only counts the row if the condition is true, ignores it otherwise

Query 06 of 08

Show transactions at Chase Downtown Chicago Feb 14, 2–4PM

Uses multiple WHERE and AND conditions to drill down to a specific branch, city, state, date and time range. Demonstrates how SQL can filter to any exact window of data.

SQL Concepts Used
2x JOINWHEREANDDATE()HOUR()BETWEENORDER BYDATE_FORMAT
Key Finding
2 Transactions Found on Valentine's Day
Hannah Phillips — Chase Downtown Chicago — In-Branch — ,581.34 — 02:59 PM — success. Olivia Martinez — Chase Downtown Chicago — ATM — 61.16 — 04:44 PM — pending.
Analysis & Insights

"This query shows the power of SQL filtering. Using WHERE and multiple AND conditions I can narrow down 10,000 transactions to find exactly which customers transacted at Chase Downtown Chicago between 2PM and 4PM on February 14th 2024. I used 2 JOINs — one to get customer names and one to get branch names. DATE() extracts just the date from the timestamp, and HOUR() extracts the hour so I can use BETWEEN 14 and 16 for 2PM to 4PM in 24-hour format. Only 2 transactions matched all conditions — this is exactly how Chase fraud investigators or branch auditors would drill into specific transaction windows."

Result Screenshot
SQL Code
USE chase_bank; SELECT t.txn_id, c.name AS customer_name, b.branch_name, b.city, t.txn_type, t.amount, t.status, DATE_FORMAT(t.txn_timestamp, '%h:%i %p') AS txn_time FROM transactions t JOIN customers c ON t.customer_id = c.customer_id JOIN branches b ON t.branch_id = b.branch_id WHERE b.branch_name = 'Chase Downtown' AND b.city = 'Chicago' AND b.state = 'IL' AND DATE(t.txn_timestamp) = '2024-02-14' AND HOUR(t.txn_timestamp) BETWEEN 14 AND 16 ORDER BY t.txn_timestamp;
Result

2 rows

Only transactions matching all 5 filter conditions — hyper specific

Business Value

Audit and investigation

Chase compliance teams use exactly this type of query to investigate suspicious activity at specific branches

Why so few results?

Very specific filters

10,000 transactions across 59 days and 10 branches — only 2 matched this exact window. More specific = fewer results.

Query 07 of 08

Who made payments at New York branches on March 1st at 11PM?

Filters by city, channel type, specific date and exact hour using multiple AND conditions. Shows after-hours payment activity in New York branches.

SQL Concepts Used
2x JOINWHEREANDDATE()HOUR() = 23ORDER BYchannel filter
Key Finding
3 Customers Made Payments at 11PM in New York
Sebastian Lewis — Chase Midtown — ATM — ,243.37 — 11:11 PM. Benjamin Martinez — Chase Times Square — In-Branch — ,187.32 — 11:17 PM. Lily Roberts — Chase Midtown — In-Branch — ,530.95 — 11:53 PM. All successful.
Analysis & Insights

"Query 7 is very similar to Query 6 but with different filters — instead of filtering by branch name I filtered by city New York, and instead of a time range I filtered to exactly 11PM using HOUR() = 23. The number 23 is because we use 24-hour format — 11PM = hour 23. I also added a channel filter to only show payment transactions, not deposits or withdrawals. I found 3 customers making payments in New York at 11PM — all successful. This kind of after-hours monitoring is important for detecting unusual payment activity or potential fraud."

Result Screenshot
SQL Code
USE chase_bank; SELECT c.name AS customer_name, b.branch_name, b.city, t.txn_type, t.channel, t.amount, t.status, DATE_FORMAT(t.txn_timestamp, '%h:%i %p') AS txn_time FROM transactions t JOIN customers c ON t.customer_id = c.customer_id JOIN branches b ON t.branch_id = b.branch_id WHERE b.city = 'New York' AND t.channel = 'payment' AND DATE(t.txn_timestamp) = '2024-03-01' AND HOUR(t.txn_timestamp) = 23 ORDER BY t.txn_timestamp;
Result

3 rows

Exactly 3 payment transactions matching all 4 filter conditions

Business Value

After-hours monitoring

Flag and review late-night payment activity — key for fraud detection and security compliance

Why HOUR() = 23?

24-hour clock in SQL

SQL uses 0–23 for hours. 11PM = hour 23. Using = 23 finds all transactions in the 11PM hour window.

Query 08 of 08

Complete transaction history for any customer

Uses two JOINs to connect transactions → customers and transactions → branches simultaneously, showing a complete spending history exactly like what Chase customer service sees when a customer calls.

SQL Concepts Used
SELECT2x JOINWHERE by nameDATE_FORMAT (full date)ORDER BY DESCmultiple table aliases (t, c, b)
Key Finding
Sebastian Lewis — Nationwide Spending Pattern
Lives in Houston but transacts across Chase Buckhead Atlanta, Chase South Beach Miami, Chase Times Square New York, Chase Downtown Chicago and more. Deposits, withdrawals, payments and transfers across all channels. This is his complete Chase story.
Analysis & Insights

"Query 8 is my most advanced query — it uses TWO JOINs at the same time. The first JOIN connects transactions to customers so I can show the customer's name and home city. The second JOIN connects transactions to branches so I can show which branch each transaction happened at and in which city. Together this gives me a complete picture of Sebastian Lewis's spending history — every transaction he made, where, when, how much and whether it succeeded. This is exactly what you see when you call Chase customer service and they pull up your account — and I built it from scratch using SQL."

Result Screenshot
SQL Code
USE chase_bank; SELECT c.name AS customer_name, c.city AS customer_city, b.branch_name, b.city AS branch_city, t.channel AS spending_type, t.txn_type, t.amount, t.status, DATE_FORMAT(t.txn_timestamp, '%M %d %Y %h:%i %p') AS date_time FROM transactions t JOIN customers c ON t.customer_id = c.customer_id JOIN branches b ON t.branch_id = b.branch_id WHERE c.name = 'Sebastian Lewis' ORDER BY t.txn_timestamp DESC;
Result

All of Sebastian's rows

Complete history — most recent transaction first

Business Value

Customer service + fraud

What Chase reps see when a customer calls. Also used for dispute resolution and fraud investigations.

Why 2 JOINs?

Two different lookups

One JOIN for customer name, one JOIN for branch name. Each needs its own ON condition matching the right ID columns.

Query 09 of 12

Which customers have less than 00 balance?

Connects customers to their accounts using JOIN, then filters for accounts with balance under 00. Identifies at-risk customers before they overdraft.

SQL Concepts Used
JOINWHERE balance < 100ORDER BY ASCaccounts table
Key Finding
3 Customers At Overdraft Risk
Addison Parker — Dallas — 5.75 🚨 | Chloe Lopez — Los Angeles — 2.25 🚨 | Scarlett Hall — Dallas — 5.50 🚨. All checking accounts. One transaction could push them into overdraft.
Analysis & Insights

"This query identifies Chase customers with less than 00 in their checking accounts — these are at overdraft risk. I used JOIN to connect customers with their account balances from the accounts table, and WHERE to filter only accounts below 00. In real banking, Chase proactively reaches out to these customers to offer overdraft protection or deposit reminders. This is a direct example of how data analytics protects both the customer and the bank."

Result Screenshot
SQL Code
USE chase_bank; SELECT c.name AS customer_name, c.account_type, c.city, a.account_number, a.account_type, a.balance FROM customers c JOIN accounts a ON c.customer_id = a.customer_id WHERE a.balance < 100 ORDER BY a.balance ASC;
Result

3 at-risk accounts

All checking accounts with balance under 00

Business Value

Overdraft prevention

Chase can proactively contact these customers before they overdraft — reduces fees and improves customer satisfaction

Why ORDER BY ASC?

Lowest balance first

ASC = ascending = smallest number first. The most at-risk customers appear at the top!

Query 10 of 12

How did transaction volume change month over month?

Groups all transactions by month, showing total transactions, unique customers, total money processed and average transaction amount for February and March 2024.

SQL Concepts Used
DATE_FORMAT %M %YCOUNTSUMAVGGROUP BY monthORDER BY MIN()
Key Finding
March Grew 6% Over February
February 2024 → 4,854 transactions → 2,452,203. March 2024 → 5,146 transactions → 3,274,233. Transaction volume grew by 292 transactions and 22,030 in revenue month over month.
Analysis & Insights

"This is a month over month trend analysis — one of the most important metrics in banking. I used DATE_FORMAT with %M %Y to group by month name and year, then counted transactions and summed amounts per month. March 2024 showed 6% growth over February — 5,146 transactions vs 4,854. This kind of trend analysis tells Chase whether the business is growing, declining or staying flat. In a real production environment this query would run automatically every month to track business performance."

Result Screenshot
SQL Code
USE chase_bank; SELECT DATE_FORMAT(txn_timestamp, '%M %Y') AS month, COUNT(txn_id) AS total_transactions, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS unique_customers, ROUND(SUM(amount), 2) AS total_amount, ROUND(AVG(amount), 2) AS avg_transaction FROM transactions GROUP BY month ORDER BY MIN(txn_timestamp);
Result

2 rows

One row per month — February and March side by side for easy comparison

Business Value

Growth tracking

Shows Chase whether transaction volume is growing month over month — core KPI for any banking business

Why ORDER BY MIN()?

Chronological order

GROUP BY loses the original order — MIN(txn_timestamp) gets the earliest date in each month to sort correctly

Query 11 of 12

Which day of the week is Chase busiest?

Uses DAYNAME to extract the day name from each transaction timestamp, then groups and ranks by transaction count to find which day of the week is most active.

SQL Concepts Used
DAYNAME()COUNTSUMAVGGROUP BY dayORDER BY DESC
Key Finding
Friday is Chase's Busiest Day — 1,550 Transactions
Friday → 1,550 | Saturday → 1,499 | Thursday → 1,470 | Monday → 1,426 | Wednesday → 1,363 | Tuesday → 1,347 | Sunday → 1,345. Customers bank before the weekend — Sunday is the quietest.
Analysis & Insights

"This query uses DAYNAME — a SQL function that converts a date into the day name like Monday, Tuesday etc. I grouped by day of week and ranked by total transactions. Friday is the busiest day with 1,550 transactions — customers do their banking before the weekend. Sunday is the quietest with only 1,345. This is extremely valuable for Chase branch managers — they need more tellers and staff on Fridays and can run maintenance on Sundays. It's a simple query but a high-impact business insight."

Result Screenshot
SQL Code
USE chase_bank; SELECT DAYNAME(txn_timestamp) AS day_of_week, COUNT(txn_id) AS total_transactions, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS unique_customers, ROUND(SUM(amount), 2) AS total_amount, ROUND(AVG(amount), 2) AS avg_transaction FROM transactions GROUP BY day_of_week ORDER BY total_transactions DESC;
Result

7 rows

One row per day of week — all 7 days ranked busiest to quietest

Business Value

Staffing optimization

Chase branch managers use day-of-week data to schedule more tellers on busy Fridays and fewer on quiet Sundays

What is DAYNAME()?

Extracts day name

DAYNAME() converts any timestamp into the full day name — Monday, Tuesday etc. No need to manually map numbers to days!

Query 12 of 12

Automatic risk scoring — who is at overdraft risk?

The most advanced query — combines JOIN, WHERE, GROUP BY and CASE WHEN to automatically categorize every Chase customer as HIGH, MEDIUM or LOW risk based on their account balance and transaction activity.

SQL Concepts Used
2x JOINCASE WHEN THEN ENDCOUNTSUMGROUP BYORDER BY ASCautomatic risk scoring
Key Finding — Risk Alert
4 HIGH RISK customers identified automatically
Addison Parker 5.75 — 210 txns 🚨 | Chloe Lopez 2.25 — 219 txns 🚨 | Scarlett Hall 5.50 — 202 txns 🚨 | Ava Thompson 50.25 — 218 txns 🚨. These customers are actively transacting despite dangerously low balances.
Analysis & Insights

"This is my most advanced and most business-critical query. It uses two JOINs — one to connect transactions to customers and one to connect customers to their account balances. Then I used CASE WHEN — SQL's IF statement — to automatically label each customer as HIGH RISK (under 00), MEDIUM RISK (under ,000) or LOW RISK based on their balance. The result ranks 50 customers by risk level. This is exactly the kind of automated risk scoring system that Chase's analytics and risk management teams build — turning raw data into actionable business intelligence."

Result Screenshot
SQL Code
USE chase_bank; SELECT c.name AS customer_name, c.city, c.account_type, a.balance AS current_balance, COUNT(t.txn_id) AS total_transactions, ROUND(SUM(t.amount), 2) AS total_spent, CASE WHEN a.balance < 500 THEN 'HIGH RISK' WHEN a.balance < 2000 THEN 'MEDIUM RISK' ELSE 'LOW RISK' END AS risk_level FROM customers c JOIN accounts a ON c.customer_id = a.customer_id JOIN transactions t ON c.customer_id = t.customer_id WHERE a.account_type = 'checking' GROUP BY c.name, c.city, c.account_type, a.balance ORDER BY a.balance ASC;
Result

50 customers scored

Every customer automatically labeled HIGH, MEDIUM or LOW risk — sorted lowest balance first

Business Value

Automated risk management

Replaces manual review — Chase can instantly identify which customers need overdraft protection outreach

What is CASE WHEN?

SQL's IF statement

CASE WHEN balance < 500 THEN 'HIGH RISK' — automatically assigns a label based on a condition. No manual work needed!

Project Summary

What I Built & What I Learned

A complete SQL analytics system built from scratch — 4 tables, 10,000 transactions, 8 queries answering real business questions about Chase Bank operations.

Closing Statement
"I didn't just build this to learn SQL."
As an Associate Banker at Chase, I see transaction patterns every single day. I built this project because I genuinely want to use data to make Chase better — to understand the numbers behind what I observe at work. Every query in this project answers a question I've actually wondered about on the job. That's exactly the mindset I want to bring as a Business Tools Analyst.
